text: The laparoscopic view shows the identification of crucial anatomical structures before the peritoneal incision, including the external iliac artery, vein, and the oblique vas deferens connecting with the testicular veins at the deep inguinal ring. The bladder's position and filling are assessed, followed by setting the horizon and framing the image for the procedure.

The surgeon grasps a thin layer of the peritoneal lamina approximately five to six centimeters laterally from the deep inguinal ring, gently incising it to allow gas entry into the preperitoneal space. The peritoneal lamina is pulled back to separate tissues in the avascular layer, with cauterization used for small visible vessels. The peritoneum is stretched away from the abdominal wall, and loose connective tissue is detached. The incision is directed towards the top of the screen, then slightly medial, and cranially near the medial umbilical ligament, while avoiding damage to the inferior epigastric vessels by sliding them over the rectus muscle.

The surgeon maintains the correct direction of the peritoneal incision, ensuring a distance of at least five to six centimeters from the hernia defect edge. The grasper in the left hand plays a dominant role in tissue dissection, providing solid peritoneal traction at a right angle. In the lateral part of the dissected space, connective and fatty tissue is removed from the peritoneum, leaving it on the abdominal wall muscles, while in the medial part, the opposite technique is applied. The bare muscle remains at the top of the screen, with fatty tissue positioned down on the bladder. The pubic symphysis is identified as a clear white dense structure located transversely, serving as a major landmark in the procedure.

The surgeon exposes Cooper's ligament, consisting of ligamentous and fascial tissues over the pubic bone, while carefully dissecting above the anterior bladder wall to avoid puncture or thermal damage. The grasper in the left hand bluntly presses down the bladder, stretching tissue fibers between the bladder wall and pubic symphysis, which are then cut under visual inspection. A small protrusion of the pubic symphysis is visible, serving as a landmark for further dissection towards the opposite side. The dissection progresses from the lateral Hasselbach space and medial Retzius space to the hernial sac and internal ring.

The surgeon isolates individual connective tissue fibers between the peritoneum and the abdominal wall, carefully cutting them using electrocauterization while avoiding damage to the parietal peritoneum. The lower flap of the peritoneum is stretched to facilitate sliding tissues from the peritoneum towards the abdominal wall. In the case of a medial direct hernia, the dissection around the inguinal ring is easier, allowing the release of the peritoneum from the vas deferens below. Solid traction behind the fatty tissue helps assess the hernia defect boundaries, revealing the white compact fibrous connective tissue of the hernia defect ring.

The surgeon applies traction to adipose tissue around the hernia defect, revealing a muff-like structure filled with adipose tissue. Gradual release of tissue adhesions around the defect exposes its complete circumference. Small vessels in the area are carefully isolated and coagulated, allowing full visualization of Cooper's ligament.

This material is a presentation of a full recording of this procedure, and only the short parts with cleaning the optics lens have been removed from the video. The playback rate is one point five times faster than an original speed of the procedure, so you can see how much time is actually needed for the following steps of this surgery.

The surgeon carefully separates tissues on the lateral edge of the direct hernia, ensuring the inferior epigastric vessels are not damaged. The entire perimeter of the abdominal wall defect within the medial hernia is visible, along with the cleansed branch of the pubic bone. The surgeon begins further separation of tissues between the bladder and the external iliac vein to prepare for placing the lower edge of the mesh without risk of folding.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lower peritoneal flap in its lateral part to create space for the lower edge of the mesh, ensuring it does not roll up during peritoneum closure. The peritoneum is carefully separated from the testicular vessels, which are gradually slid away from the peritoneum. Near the vas deferens, the pretransverse fascia is sharply cut, releasing and allowing further separation of the vas deferens. The surgeon uses a grasper and scissors to effectively separate the tissues.

The surgeon carefully grasps the pre-bladder adipose tissue and pulls it medially away from the external iliac vein. This maneuver helps identify the thinnest area of adipose tissue for separation. Electrocautery is used to cauterize the fibers before cutting to prevent fluid accumulation from small lymph vessels. This process is repeated to gradually free the space.

The surgeon releases the upper flap of the peritoneum to create a small pocket for the superior edge of the synthetic mesh. Using two instruments, the peritoneum is grasped and pulled tangentially to the abdominal cavity surface, separating it from the abdominal wall. The flap is passed medially between instruments, and the synthetic mesh is inserted into the preperitoneal pocket.

The surgeon uses a lightweight three-dimensional polypropylene mesh to repair the direct hernia. The mesh is carefully positioned with appropriate margins around the defect to ensure stability, even after bladder filling. The lower edge of the mesh is precisely placed to prevent folding by the peritoneum or bladder. The lower flap of the peritoneum is lifted upwards without disturbing the mesh's position, and the hernial sac peritoneum is pulled out to prevent internal strangulation.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using an absorbable suture, completing the procedure. Gas is desquamated from the preperitoneal space to ensure the mesh remains in its correct position.
